planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring as soil becomes workable, in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade; Astilbe biternata is the only astilbe native to North America and establishes best with consistent moisture during its first season.

directions: Plant crown divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic woodland soil in partial shade, with the crown set just at or slightly below the soil surface. Astilbe biternata is the only species of astilbe native to North America, occurring naturally in the moist, rich coves and wooded slopes of the southern Appalachians, where it produces bold, bi- to tri-ternate foliage and large, creamy-white plume flowers from June to July — far larger and more architectural than most Asian astilbes. Keep evenly moist throughout the growing season; drought causes premature browning of leaf margins and reduces flowering. Divide clumps every 3–4 years in early spring to maintain vigor, as older crowns can die out at the center.

planting.note: Plant crown divisions in early spring in moist, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; this small Japanese astilbe is ideally suited to the front of shaded borders and woodland edges, where its compact habit and glossy, simple leaves provide year-round textural interest.

directions: Plant crown divisions in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil with the crown set just at soil level; this small Japanese species is distinguished from other astilbes by its undivided (simple or shallowly lobed), glossy dark-green leaves — an unusual trait in the genus. Astilbe simplicifolia produces graceful, arching sprays of star-shaped pink or white flowers in midsummer, and has been widely used as a parent of many popular dwarf hybrid astilbes valued for their compact size and refined foliage. Keep consistently moist and apply a mulch to protect shallow roots from summer heat and winter freeze-thaw cycles. Divide every 3 years in spring to prevent crown congestion and sustain flowering.

planting.note: Plant divisions in early spring in deep, cool, humus-rich, consistently moist soil in full to partial shade; Deinanthe bifida is a rare Japanese woodland perennial that requires protection from afternoon sun and drying winds, and rewards patient cultivation with unusual nodding flowers and bold textured foliage.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, deep, humus-rich, evenly moist soil in full shade, enriched with leaf mold or compost; Deinanthe bifida is a rare and prized collector's plant in the hydrangea family (Hydrangeaceae), native to the shaded mountain woodlands of Japan, where it produces bold, deeply textured, wrinkled leaves and nodding, cupped white to pale-blue flowers in summer that are unlike any other shade perennial in appearance. It requires consistent moisture and cool root conditions — mulch heavily to maintain soil temperature and moisture through summer. Protect from deer and slugs, and avoid disturbing established clumps, which resent division and are slow to re-establish.

planting.note: Plant rosette divisions or offsets in spring, wedged into shaded rock crevices or a gritty, humus-amended, sharply drained substrate in full shade; Haberlea rhodopensis is a remarkable resurrection plant from the Rhodope Mountains of Bulgaria and Greece that can survive complete desiccation and revive fully upon rewatering.

directions: Plant rosette offsets into shaded north- or east-facing rock crevices or in a very gritty, sharply drained mix of equal parts loam, coarse grit, and leaf mold, ensuring the crown sits above the soil line to prevent rot; Haberlea rhodopensis is one of very few vascular plants capable of surviving complete desiccation — a trait known as poikilochlorophylly — and will fully revive from a desiccated state when moisture returns. It produces compact rosettes of wrinkled, spoon-shaped leaves and bears clusters of pale lilac, tubular flowers on short stems in spring; excellent drainage is non-negotiable, as the crown rots in poorly drained or wet winter conditions. Once established, it requires minimal supplemental watering and thrives with the reflected cool humidity of a shaded stone face.

planting.note: Plant rosette divisions in spring into north- or east-facing shaded rock crevices or a sharply drained, humus-rich, gritty medium; Ramonda myconi is a Pyrenean cliff perennial that requires excellent drainage and cool, shaded conditions to perform well, and is considered one of the finest miniature shade perennials for the rock garden.

directions: Plant rosette offsets tilted slightly sideways into shaded rock crevices or in a gritty alpine mix to prevent water from pooling in the crown, which causes rot; Ramonda myconi is a charming miniature rosette perennial endemic to the shaded, north-facing limestone cliffs of the Pyrenees, producing crinkled, dark-green, hairy leaves and bearing remarkably beautiful violet-mauve flowers with a bright yellow anther cone in spring that strongly resemble African violets — to which it is distantly related in the family Gesneriaceae. Like its close relative Haberlea rhodopensis, Ramonda is also a resurrection plant capable of surviving periods of desiccation. It requires cold winter humidity and cool summer conditions, and performs best in climates with cool, moist winters and mild summers; it is not suited to hot, humid summer regions.
